text_blocks.text: The mechanisms used to evade the
adaptive immune system are more
complicated. The simplest approach is to
rapidly change non-essential epitopes (amino
acids and/or sugars) on the surface of the
pathogen, while keeping essential epitopes
concealed. This is called antigenic variation.
An example is HIV, which mutates rapidly, so
the proteins on its viral envelope that are
essential for entry into its host target cell
are constantly changing. These frequent
changes in antigens may explain the failures
of vaccines directed at this virus. The
parasiteTrypanosoma brucei uses a similar
strategy, constantly switching one type of
surface protein for another, allowing it to
stay one step ahead of the antibody
response. Masking antigens with host
molecules is another common strategy for
avoiding detection by the immune system. In
HIV, the envelope that covers the virion is
formed from the outermost membrane of
the host cell; such "self-cloaked" viruses make
it difficult for the immune system to identify
them as "non-self" structures.
